data.title: ASML: Post Q3 Earnings Selloff Appears Overdone (Ratings Upgrade)
data.description:
Post 3Q Earnings Pullback Presents A Buying Opportunity

ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) stock dropped nearly 22% following unexpectedly lower demand, driven by slower ramp of new nodes at certain customers according to management's comments. While the company topped both revenue and non-GAAP EPS estimates, it reported a 53% sequential decline in bookings to €2.63 billion in 3Q FY2024, which was 51% below consensus. Bookings are a key growth metric for ASML as they reflect the total backlog trajectory, and the backlog eventually translates into future revenue. During the 3Q earnings call, the CEO stated, "the recovery is more gradual than previously expected", reflecting customer cautiousness. As a result, the company’s FY2025 revenue outlook also falls short of estimates. ASML lowered the high end of its FY2025 revenue guidance from €40 billion to €35 billion, with the midpoint missing estimates by 9.6%.

Additionally, the weak order intake will reduce down payments and lead to a significant inventory buildup. ASML's inventories on the balance sheet have increased significantly over the recent quarters. This will negatively impact the company’s FCF profile in the near term. Management expects inventory levels to normalize once the business rebounds.

In my previous analysis, I initiated a hold rating on ASML on July 12th, citing uncertainty around the size of the TSMC deal and high exposure to China, all while trading at an elevated valuation. Since then, the stock has dropped 38%, underperforming the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) by 26%. While I had previously anticipated a growth turnaround for ASML in FY2025, driven by strong EUV sales, this optimism has been dampened by the weak guidance.

It’s important to note that, due to lofty valuation in the semiconductor sector, these stocks are heavily reliant on future growth expectations. Investors are betting that demand for AI chips will continue to sustain the current growth trajectory. Therefore, any unexpected slowdown in chip demand will likely trigger a significant pullback, primarily due to multiple contraction in valuations. However, I believe ASML is getting more attractive after 22% drop in two days as valuation has largely come down since my last rating, reflecting some of near-term headwind have been priced in. Therefore, I'm upgrading the stock to buy.

Weak EUV Growth Temporarily Pressures Margins Expansion

ASML also lowered its margin outlook for 4Q FY2024 and FY2025. While its 4Q revenue nearly in-line with the consensus, its 4Q gross margin is expected to be in a range of 49% to 50%, below the consensus of 50.5%. As shown in the chart, this is expected to be the lowest since 2Q FY2022. In addition, considering its "R&D costs of around €1.1 billion and SG&A costs of around €300 million" and the midpoint of gross margin guidance, we can calculate that its operating margin in 4Q FY2024 would be 33.9%, which shows slightly improvement from 33.2% seen in 4Q FY2023.

Meanwhile, they also lowered its FY2025 gross margin by 3% to the midpoint of 52% as revenue outlook disappoints, below the estimates of 53.9%. This prevents ASML to from breaking its previous 52.7% high seen in FY2021. The management previously aimed to achieve gross margin of 54% to 56% during Investor Day of 2022. During the 3Q FY2024 call, they highlighted a key driver on gross margin expansion was relying on EUV Low NA (high margin), with less than 50 EUV Low NA shipments expected in FY2025. In terms of EUV High NA, the company expects more units to be recognized in FY2025, which will have a dilutive effect on gross margin. However, they also indicated that gross margin on EUV High NA will improve next year due to higher production efficiency. Therefore, I believe the margin drop is temporary and will gradually bounce back in 2H FY2025.

Potential Slower Demand In China?

While management only mentioned that the unexpected large drop in bookings was driven by lower production of new nodes at "certain customers" in the Logic segment, they did not provide further details, which remains a key question for me. As shown in the chart, China is still ASML's largest customer, with management expecting around 20% of FY2025 total sales to come from China and showing more "normalized percentage" going forward. It’s difficult to speculate whether the sharp decline in bookings is related to China exposure. However, due to ongoing export curbs between the U.S. and China, the company has previously mentioned in 1Q FY2024 earnings call that 10% to 15% sales could be impacted by China. Therefore, this is not news to investors. Nevertheless, ASML may face more structural demand headwinds, especially as the next administration takes office. However, I expect a normalized exposure in China might settle at below 20% over time.

Valuation Is Getting Attractive

Following a 22% pullback in two trading days due to the disappointing bookings result, ASML's valuation multiple has come down even further. While the currently multiple is not "dirt cheap", I believe the risk and reward are becoming more attractive as the company is still expected to see a growth and margin turnaround in FY2025. ASML's GAAP P/E TTM is currently at 37x, significantly lower than the 56x at the time of my last rating. Considering its non-GAAP EPS fwd of €29.7 based on the Seeking Alpha consensus, its non-GAAP P/E fwd multiple will come down to 22.9x, which is close to Lam Research (LRCX)'s 21.3x, KLA Corporation (KLAC)'s 23.8x, and Applied Materials (AMAT)'s 22.4x. Additionally, most of ASML's valuation multiples are currently 10% below their 5-year averages. Therefore, while ASML's growth and margin outlook for FY2025 is weaker than expected, its growth acceleration and margin expansion story remain intact for FY2025.

Bottom Line

While ASML's 3Q FY2024 earnings revealed disappointing bookings results, leading to a downward revision in FY2025 revenue and margin guidance, the stock's sharp pullback indicates a buying opportunity. This decline may have been impacted by potential headwinds related to China and U.S. export restrictions. I believe ASML’s long-term demand growth for EUV shipments remains intact. Investors should monitor the company’s inventory build-up to gauge the pace of demand recovery in coming earnings releases. Moreover, valuation multiples have compressed significantly and are now below ASML's 5-year average, aligning with those of its peers. Given that ASML is still well-positioned for a turnaround in FY2025, the current valuation is attractive enough to be bullish. Therefore, I'm upgrading ASML to a buy, as the recent selloff has pushed the stock into oversold territory.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ALPS Smith Core Plus Bond ETF Approaches Its First Birthday
data.description:
Introduction

This December, the ALPS Smith Core Plus Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SMTH) will celebrate its launching. While the firm has 12 fixed income funds, this is the newest of their three ETFs, and its first with the ALPS Smith management team. Stretching one's portfolio duration makes sense when interest rates are heading down. This ETF provides that and has treated investors better than the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), which I compare SMTH against in this article.

While its history is under a year, I like its results so far to go against my normal Hold rating for new ETFs and award SMTH a Buy rating.

ALPS Smith Core Plus Bond ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as:

The Smith Core Plus Bond ETF is an exchange traded fund launched and managed by ALPS Advisors, Inc. The fund is co-managed by Smith Capital Investors, LLC. It invests in the fixed income markets. The fund invests in bonds including government notes and bonds, corporate bonds, convertible bonds, commercial and residential mortgage-backed securities, and zero-coupon bonds. It employs fundamental analysis with bottom-up securities picking approach to create its portfolio. It seeks to benchmark the performance of its portfolio against the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. ALPS ETF Trust - Alps | Smith Core Plus Bond ETF was formed on December 5, 2023 and is domiciled in the United States.

Source: seekingalpha SMTH

SMTH has $968m in AUM and charges 59bps in fees; not excessive for an actively managed bond ETF. Using payments so far, the Forward yield should be around 4.3%. The managers provide this investment objective and strategy:

The ALPS | Smith Core Plus Bond ETF seeks above average total return from a combination of current income and capital appreciation.

The ALPS | Smith Core Plus Bond ETF pursues its investment objective by investing, under normal circumstances, at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in bonds. The Fund seeks to generate total return from a combination of current income and capital appreciation.

Source: alpsfunds SMTH

Holdings review

Starting with sectors, we see 57% are either direct Government debt or mortgages back by US agencies.

As for ratings, despite the portfolio's largest sector being US Government bonds, AAA weighting is shown as 0%.

Morningstar shows the same ratings data and gives the SMTH portfolio an average rating of A, whereas the AGG ETF gets a rating of AA-. Since US governments are the top 15 holdings, I will show the top holdings in each of the three largest sector allocations.

Overall, SMTH has an Effective Duration of 6.5 years; Effective Maturity of 10.3 years, and a YTM of 5.4%. With over 650 positions and no issue except USTs over 1% of the portfolio's weight, any defaults would have minimal effect. With under 7% rated below investment grade, again, default risk is very low.

The next table shows when the holdings mature, with the WAM being just over 10 years.

With ST rates now on a downward slope, having 23% needing replacement over the next three years could dampen the payouts. I have seen industry experts state that 7-10 years on the curve is the "sweet spot" and SMTH has an 18% allocation with that maturity time frame.

Distributions review

Even before the FFOC cut the FFR in September, investors have seen lower payouts decline, with the peak being back in March of this year. More reductions should be expected as forecasts are for up to 2% more in cuts over the next two years in the FFR as long as inflation stays under control. All payouts so far have come from income.

SMTH ETF compared to AGG ETF

The AGG ETF invests based on the index that the SMTH ETF measures its performance against, making it a good fund to compare SMTH against. The AGG ETF is probably considered the "gold standard" for US bond market coverage. This is how Seeking Alpha describes this ETF:

The investment seeks to track the investment results of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. The index measures the performance of the total U.S. investment-grade bond market. The fund will invest at least 80% of its assets in the component securities of the underlying index and TBAs that have economic characteristics that are substantially identical to the economic characteristics of the component securities of the underlying index, and the fund will invest at least 90% of its assets in fixed income securities of the types included in the underlying index that the advisor believes will help the fund track the underlying index.

Source: seekingalpha AGG

AGG has $119b in AUM with a Forward yield of 3.5%. As might be expected, its low 3bps cost gives its investors a huge annual advantage over SMTH. Here is an overview of the index:

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, fixed-rate agency MBS, ABS and CMBS (agency and non-agency).

Source: assets.bbhub.io

Factor SMTH ETF AGG ETF
AUM $970m $118b
Fees 59bps 3bps
Yield 4.3% 3.5%
Eff Duration (yrs) 6.5 6.1
Eff Maturity (yrs) 10.3 8.4
Avg Coupon 5.1% 3.5%
YTM 5.4% 4.4%
Avg Price $102.11 $95.27

Before looking at risk and return results, I like what SMTH is offering in terms of a superior yield and longer duration as we enter a period of declining interest rates. The much higher average coupon offsets the fact that SMTH's average price is over Par, whereas AGG has an average bond price below Par.

The following data for the Jan-Sep 2024 period.

During this time frame, SMTH had a better return and lower StdDev and Drawdown. Those facts are reflected in both the Sharpe and Sortino ratios, where SMTH's value is more than twice what AGG achieved. While I won't give the AGG ETF a Sell rating, between this pair of bond ETFs, SMTH gets a Buy rating for executing a successful active strategy.

Portfolio strategy

While the latest jobs report might slow the pace of FOMC rate cuts, we have passed the peak, which means stretching one's portfolio's duration is a strategy to consider. ETFs with a long effective duration and maturity values, what the long end of the yield curve is doing is more important than the short end.

While not shown, the 10-yr yield has been declining steadily since 1985. Currently, yields match both the early part of this century and what we saw before the inflationary 1970s arrived. One interpretation is rates at the long-end might not have much more to decline, especially if the US Government annual budget deficits continue, which would put upward pressure on the rates investors demanded for the extra risk of a ratings downgrade. Investors fearing such a scenario might want to stick with bond funds with durations under 4 years or even shorter, of which there are plenty to pick from.

data.title: Investing In An Era Of Flouted Rules
data.description:
By Jeffrey Rosenberg & Tom Parker

The unique characteristics of the post-pandemic economy have brought about an era of flouted rules. Despite a series of historically reliable indicators pointing to a recession, the economy has so far continued to defy expectations. A recent string of more solid economic data has led to another reassessment of those recession fears and market expectations for aggressive interest rate cuts.

Economic resilience calls into question the level of policy restrictiveness and the potential for easing financial conditions to influence the transmission of monetary policy. Coupled with fiscal policy uncertainty and escalating tensions in the Middle East, these dynamics are among the potential challenges facing bond investors as we look ahead.

An era of flouted rules

We are investing in an era of flouted rules. The tendency to blindly follow these rules has led investors towards prematurely de-risking and over-estimating the likelihood of recession. Historically reliable, leading recession indicators including surveys of manufacturing and service purchasing managers turned recessionary nearly two years ago. Similarly, the yield curve inversion as well as our preferred modified version of that indicator—the aggregate spread,which includes the excess of inflation over unemployment to that inversion—began signaling a recession over two years ago. Partly referencing these rules and inputs, consensus views on recession probabilities peaked last year around this time. They subsequently fell all throughout Q4 of last year and into this year.

The unique characteristics of the post-pandemic economy and recovery lie to blame for most of the ahistoric economic experience that has pushed back on these historically derived recession indicators. Fed Chair Jay Powell referenced this dynamic in the July Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) press conference stating,

“Let’s remember that this pandemic era has been one in which so many… apparent rules have been flouted… because this situation really is unusual or unique in that so much of this inflation came from the shutdown of the economy and the resulting supply problems in the face of, admittedly, very strong demand.”

The outcome of these flouted rules has been a consistently lower estimate of expected growth by economists and the market that has needed to be revised upwards since Q4 2022 (Figure 1).

Given these dynamics, it might be surprising that another rule, the Sahm Rule—stating that a 50bps increase in the three-month average of the unemployment rate above its 12-month low predicts all historical recessions—wasn’t met with a more skeptical greeting.

Nevertheless, it coincided with a slowdown in payrolls, a rise in initial jobless claims, and a preliminary benchmark revision of establishment survey data which revised down twelve months’ worth of establishment payrolls by 818,000, or just under 70,000 per month. This was substantially greater than the ~300,000 expected downward revision. In aggregate, these dynamics contributed to increased fears of recession and expectations for Fed interest rate cuts. At the time in early August, the revisions added to the notion of both a slowing and overstatedly strong labor market— something that Powell explicitly acknowledged in his September FOMC press conference.

Hoping to temper expectations for even deeper cuts, which at the time the bond market had already begun aggressively pricing, Fed Governor Christopher Waller’s speech titled “The Time Has Come” tried to lay out expectations both for the size of the first cut and the committee’s thought process on the path ahead. He stated, “I believe we should be data dependent, but not overreact to any data point, including the latest data.” In doing so, he addressed the market’s preoccupation with recession risk signaled by rules such as the Sahm Rule, citing three key reasons to not blindly follow these recession rules:1

  1. Rules are nothing more than a mechanical, statistical description of past economic outcomes. They do not seek to explain what economic forces drive the relationship between data, nor are they based on the totality of economic data.
  2. Recessions occur when a major shock hits the economy. In the absence of a large negative shock, an inversion of the yield curve or a triggering of the Sahm Rule doesn’t necessarily mean we are entering a recession.
  3. Recession rules typically pick up demand-driven recessions. Most of the increase in the unemployment rate is from workers entering the labor force and not finding jobs right away. As a result, the recent rise in the unemployment rate appears to be more of a supply- driven phenomenon rather than demand-driven.

To add to these points, Figure 2 illustrates what we call the “Augmented Sahm Rule” which includes corporate profit margins alongside the rising unemployment rate. In doing so, we attempt to address the third point in Waller’s critique—that increasing unemployment best predicts a recession when it’s demand-driven rather than supply- driven. By adding in corporate margins, we can see that in almost all cases of rising unemployment predicting the onset of a recession, it is also accompanied by falling corporate margins. Falling corporate margins can signal a decline in demand is leading to the shedding of workers that indicates the onset of a recession more accurately than rising unemployment on its own.

As Figure 2 highlights, the big outlier in the current environment is corporate margins are rising not falling. Now, that characteristic itself may be a victim of the same post-pandemic issues plaguing these other rules. As the post-pandemic nature of corporate margins and profitability displays a high degree of concentration, a small number of companies may be holding up the picture of aggregate profitability—masking greater vulnerability beneath the surface. The dichotomy of performance between large cap and small cap stocks suggests that this is a possibility.

Nevertheless, the overall stronger economic picture and the general interpretation of supply-side rather than demand- side considerations likely accounts for the greater rationale behind the rise in the unemployment rate. And although exceptionally volatile month-to-month in the household survey, the recent tick down in the unemployment rate to nearly rounding down to 4.0% (4.05% unrounded) also suggests that fears over the Sahm Rule predicting a recession again appear to be unfounded.

How restrictive is policy?

With payroll growth post the September jobs report (released October 4th) now averaging 186,000 over the past three months, initial jobless claims again falling (and appearing to have previously increased based on residual seasonality rather than signal), and the substantial upward recent revisions to Gross Domestic Income (GDI), both recession fears and Fed cut expectations are being rapidly reassessed.

Preliminary GDI releases had been showing growth at less than half the estimates of GDP. That lent credence to concerns that growth was being overstated, suggesting that future growth would falter as savings were being depleted at a rapid pace. However, initial measurements in the national accounts can be significantly revised as new and more complete data comes in. In the case of Figure 3, the revisions completely changed the contour of the savings rate, and the potential support for future consumption from a consumer that no longer appears so over-extended.

Though rising unemployment may yet signal an eventual recession as it has in past cycles, the “era of flouted rules” scenarios suggest that the uniqueness of post-pandemic economics may be more at play. If so, an additional consideration should be given to the restrictiveness of current monetary policy, as the belief in both sufficiently restrictive and even overly-restrictive policy stands behind FOMC participants’ forecasts for nearly 200bps of policy rate reductions by the end of next year. The Fed and FOMC participants have said much about the need for policy normalization due to progress on inflation, the desire to avoid a hard-landing, and the need to recalibrate policy before labor markets weakness accelerates.

Less has been mentioned about the role of financial conditions (and even less about the role of the Fed’s balance sheet) in the transmission of monetary policy. The persistence of above-potential growth suggests several possibilities: the degree of interest sensitivity in the economy is lower post-pandemic, the neutral rate of interest is higher post-pandemic, or the impact of signaling a desire to cut rates back to a pre-pandemic sense of normal has led to much easier financial conditions than would be expected given the current policy rate (Figure 4). All of these may be at play, accounting for less restrictive policy.

Middle East uncertainty + Fiscal policy uncertainty = Hedging efficacy uncertainty

Middle east uncertainty adds to a theme we have discussed before: the uncertainty in the future efficacy of bonds as a hedge during the next major stock downturn. In prior quarterly outlooks, we have discussed how the fiscal policy backdrop—with a significantly higher level of debt and deficits in today’s world—requires greater Treasury debt issuance. With the Fed’s balance sheet policies no longer as expansionary as they were post-Global Financial Crisis (GFC) or post-COVID, and as the Treasury finds itself unable to surge T-bill issuance to absorb the excess, private market participants may find themselves being asked to finance a substantially larger amount of debt than in prior cycles. At the same time, the premium for holding longer maturity debt remains depressed by historical comparisons. These dynamics could lead to greater pressure for higher yields and wider term premiums on longer maturity debt. This can result in differing bond performance by maturity, with shorter maturity yields falling more in line with a recessionary Fed easing cycle. Consequently, longer-term debt may underperform as a diversifier for equity exposure.

Increases in oil prices related to Middle East uncertainty represent another potential challenge. Falling headline inflation had supported yield levels and declining inflation concerns in the near-term. Most of that decline can be traced to supply considerations rather than demand considerations with OPEC signaling the potential for scrapping production limits outweighing the impact of potential demand growth from China stimulus and US growth resilience.

However, the potential for war-related supply disruptions undercuts those previous trends with rising prices reflecting some of those potential future supply impacts.

For bonds, that presents a challenging prospect of risk-off with rising headline inflation. Though the Fed tends to look through short-term increases in food and energy prices, instead favoring core measures of inflation, the impact on the longer-term versus shorter-term interest rate reaction could be further differentiated by the conflicting forces of potential Fed cuts due to demand destruction (from an oil price shock, financial conditions tightening) and the longer- term impact of rising headline inflation making the “last mile” of reaching the 2% inflation target that much more difficult.

This suggests a different degree of hedging efficacy based on maturity as we have seen in several recent “risk off” episodes for equities. Figure 5 highlights the correlation of stock and bond returns by bond maturity. In each period of correlations declining, corresponding with equities declining, the strongest bond performance was led by the shortest maturities. This is a traditional “flight to quality” response where shorter maturity yields decline more than longer maturities, which is more typical of bond market performance when the Fed funds rate is further from zero. It is also something we may see more of in an environment of oil price induced risk off. This is something for investors to consider when looking for ballast in bond portfolios.

Conclusion

The Sahm Rule may be the latest in a series of “flouted rules” following stronger-than-expected labor market data. The unique characteristics of the post-pandemic economy have continued to push back on long-standing recession indicators so far, leading investors to recalibrate expectations for significant rate cuts.

Less attention has been given to the question of how restrictive monetary policy really is, with FOMC participants forecasting nearly 200bps of policy rate reductions by the end of next year.

The impact of easing financial conditions on the transmission of monetary policy could constrain the Fed’s ability to deliver on those cuts, potentially challenging bond market performance.

At the same time, investors may want to consider the implications of fiscal policy challenges and conflict in the Middle East on bond performance and hedging efficacy across the maturity spectrum which may matter more for portfolio construction as we look ahead.

This material is prepared by BlackRock and is not intended to be relied upon as a forecast, research or investment advice, and is not a recommendation, offer or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. The opinions expressed are as of October 2024 and may change as subsequent conditions vary. The information and opinions contained in this material are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy. As such, no warranty of accuracy or reliability is given and no responsibility arising in any other way for errors and omissions (including responsibility to any person by reason of negligence) is accepted by BlackRock, its officers, employees or agents.

This material may contain “forward-looking” information that is not purely historical in nature. Such information may include, among other things, projections and forecasts. There is no guarantee that any of these views will come to pass. Reliance upon information in this material is at the sole discretion of the reader. This material is intended for information purposes only and does not constitute investment advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell in any securities, BlackRock funds or any investment strategy nor shall any securities be offered or sold to any person in any jurisdiction in which an offer, solicitation, purchase or sale would be unlawful under the securities laws of such jurisdiction.

Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending upon market conditions. The two main risks related to fixed income investing are interest rate risk and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in the market value of bonds. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the issuer of the bond will not be able to make principal and interest payments. The principal on mortgage- or asset-backed securities may be prepaid at any time, which will reduce the yield and market value of these securities. Obligations of US Government agencies and authorities are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the US Government. Investments in non-investment-grade debt securities (“high-yield bonds” or “junk bonds”) may be subject to greater market fluctuations and risk of default or loss of income and principal than securities in higher rating categories. Income from municipal bonds may be subject to state and local taxes and at times the alternative minimum tax.

Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Investing involves risk, including possible loss of principal.

©2024 BlackRock. Inc. or its affiliates. All rights reserved. BLACKROCK and ALADDIN are trademarks of BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All other trademarks are the property of their respective owners.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Discover Financial Services 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Discover Financial Services in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: The 6 ETFs I'll Be Buying In 2025 (And You Might Want To Buy Them Too)
data.description:
Several readers asked me for an article highlighting my favorite ETFs for 2025 and beyond.

So, let me quickly walk you through the six ETFs that will make up 50% to 60% of my ZEUS Family Portfolio next year.

Why Own ETFs At All? Aren't Stocks Better?

Even if you have a great way to analyze companies and valuations and pick stocks like a champion, there are still important risks to owning individual companies, so you should consider ETFs.

For one thing, any company can go to zero, and even if they don't go bankrupt, almost half of all US stocks since 1980 suffered what JPMorgan calls "permanent catastrophic returns."

In other words, they fell 70%-plus and never recovered, not even after 30-40 years.

AAA Rated Companies Have A 1 in 1,429 Chance Of Going Bankrupt In The Next 30 Years

It might surprise you that even AAA-rated companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) have a slight but real chance of going to zero.

In other words, if you buy an individual stock, and it falls 70%-plus, or buy a company in a major bear market, there's a 14% to 65% chance you're buying a value trap, where the fundamentals have broken permanently, something that won't be obvious for several years.

In contrast, ETFs, if they're well-designed (like index funds), are "risk-free" in the sense that a diversified portfolio (like the S&P 500) can't go to zero without an apocalypse.

  • Goldman Sachs estimates a 2.5% risk of nuclear war in any given year and the "S&P becoming a total loss."

In other words, while the stock market is generally risk-free as long as America's economy survives, individual companies have fundamental bankruptcy or thesis-breaking risks. You must be careful about what companies you buy from and why.

How My Family Portfolio Uses ETFs

My family portfolio focuses on maximizing long-term income because we're targeting a 6.5% long-term annual withdrawal rate.

  • 5% to charity.
  • 0.5% gifts to family/friends.
  • 1% for living expenses (future wife and kids).

Adding a company or ETF to my portfolio must be additive to the portfolio goals.

For example, a company like British American (BTI) yielding 8% with 5% long-term growth guidance means 13% long-term return potential and income growth.

Thus, BTI helps boost the portfolio's yield and keeps up with its historical 13% to 14% returns (since 1990).

What about most dividend ETFs?

They fall short of the portfolio's historical or future consensus return potential because dividend ETFs like SCHD (high-yield blue chip) or dividend growth (like DGRW) have 10% to 12% long-term consensus return potential.

  • Yield + long-term growth consensus for their portfolios.

In other words, they aren't worth adding because they would merely add diversification for their own sake rather than boosting the portfolio's fundamentals.

But those are dividend-focused ETFs; the same isn't true among hedging or growth ETFs.

The Benefits Of Growth And Hedging ETFs

  • KMLM: 5 Reasons Now Is The Time To Buy This 8% Yielding ETF

The first ETF I'll buy more of next year is KMLM, the long-time king of hedging strategies.

The Mount Lucas managed futures ETF (KMLM) is a core holding for me because it has the best historical hedging track record of any hedging strategy since 1988.

Compared to bonds, the MLM index has generated 5X better average gains in bear markets and the same average returns (but higher median returns) than long bonds.

However, unlike bonds, which are always "risk off," the MLM index is dynamic and adapts to whatever economy or market conditions exist.

In other words, the hedging power of long bonds, but no crushing losses in a rising rate environment.

ETF I'm Buying In 2025 #2: Simplify Managed Futures ETF (CTA)

  • CTA: 5 Reasons I Added This 7.6% Yielding ETF To My Portfolio

Simplify's managed futures ETF has three attractive features, earning it the most significant position in my portfolio (26%).

  1. Quarterly dividends.
  2. Two asset model (management says it outperforms four asset models, like most managed futures in backtests).
  3. Includes carry trade (relative value + trend).

KMLM uses three asset classes (bonds, currencies, and commodities) to avoid getting trapped by stock market bubbles that pop with spectacular results.

Dynamic Beta Managed Futures (DBMF) is a popular "Vanguard of managed futures" ETF design replicating the Soc Gen CTA index.

That means it uses stocks, bonds, commodities, and currencies.

If stocks are trending up (like 2024), DBMF goes long and keeps getting longer until the trend reverses.

However, the result can be shocking when there's a market correction (-9.7% decline from July 16 to Aug. 5).

DBMF is owned as a hedge, increasing when the market falls.

However, corrections can result in sharper declines when there's no time for trend-following funds like DBMF to get short stocks.

In other words, you buy managed futures to go up in falling markets. But if they have stock futures exposure, they can increase volatility, failing to do their jobs.

In short, corrections-managed futures aren't guaranteed to go up.

  • Non-correlation to stock market = sometimes they go up and sometimes down in a modest downturn.

However, you can see how avoiding stock exposure like KMLM and CTA (by design) helps avoid nasty surprises like those in many managed futures funds.

The 9.7% Summer Pullback: July 16 to Aug. 5

AHLT is the largest managed futures ETF by assets under management. Imagine owning an ETF and thinking it will go up during most market declines, but thanks to stocks being so hot this year, the decline was 50% larger.

So, Simplify did well (it was this year's No. 1 managed futures fund), but what about the long term?

Unfortunately, we don't have the kind of transparency that KMLM does with its rules-based pure trend approach.

MLM discloses the exact rules for how it works, creating transparency (plus a 38-year historical track record) that gives investors confidence that KMLM will perform as it's designed to in the long term.

In contrast, Simplify uses Altis Partners to manage its managed futures ETF via a proprietary strategy that includes trend following, carry (relative yield futures), and two other methods designed to prevent the kind of significant crashes that can occur with trend following when trends suddenly reverse.

For example, CTA was the second best-performing managed futures fund during the regional banking crisis.

And then suddenly, bonds went from stone-cold to red-hot (biggest weekly bond rally since 1982), and trend followers lost 10% in a matter of days.

Simplify, whose proprietary "black box" algorithm is a mystery, was supposed to avoid a major crash. It failed to do so.

CTA fell 16% in a matter of days and recovered quickly.

However, it has the worst decline of any managed futures fund, which means that anyone who wants to own this fund should likely size their position with this significant black swan risk in mind.

  • KMLM fell 4%, 4X less than CTA.
  • KMLM had the most minor declines of any managed futures fund.

I combine KMLM and CTA as the core of my family portfolio's hedging basket.

Simplify Has Been Beating KMLM Since Inception, Possibly Indicating A New King Of Hedging Strategies

Down Months For S&P

CTA KMLM DBMF S&P 500 CTA/KMLM CTA/KMLM/DBMF
Year Month Return Return Return Return Return Return
2022 9 -0.08% 3.98% 5.77% -9.22% 1.95% 3.22%
2022 4 8.96% 9.75% 10.59% -8.73% 9.36% 9.77%
2022 6 3.47% -1.44% 3.48% -8.27% 1.02% 1.84%
2022 12 -1.42% 1.55% 0.35% -5.77% 0.07% 0.16%
2023 9 8.99% 4.63% 4.63% -4.78% 6.81% 6.08%
2024 4 9.78% 4.18% 4.36% -4.10% 6.98% 6.11%
2022 8 4.23% 8.69% 2.72% -4.09% 6.46% 5.21%
2023 2 9.28% 1.75% 0.75% -2.45% 5.52% 3.93%
2023 10 -4.10% -0.68% -0.38% -2.12% -2.39% -1.72%
2023 8 -1.13% 1.47% 0.33% -1.60% 0.17% 0.22%
Average 3.80% 3.39% 3.26% -5.11% 3.59% 3.48%
Median 3.85% 2.87% 3.10% -4.44% 3.73% 3.58%
Best 9.78% 9.75% 10.59% -1.60% 9.36% 9.77%
Worst -4.10% -1.44% -0.38% -9.22% -2.39% -1.72%
S&P Correlation -0.12 -0.28 -0.74 1.00 -0.21 -0.40

(Source: Portfolio Visualizer)

CTA came out in March 2022, meaning limited historical returns, but what we have is impressive.

In the average down month for stocks, the S&P fell 5% while CTA rose 3.8% with a median gain of 3.9%.

That's slightly better than KMLM's 3.4% gain and DBMF's 3.3% gain.

CTA's worst monthly return in a down month for stocks was -4%, which is far worse than KMLM or DBMF.

In other words, CTA appears to have benefits but downsides that investors should be aware of, including a "black box" strategy that management will never disclose.

Historical Returns Since April 2022

CTA is more volatile than DBMF or KMLM, but this is by design.

  • Two non-correlated asset clashes instead of three or four.

It's designed to provide superior long-term returns, and thus far, it has outperformed its ETF peers.

The only managed futures fund that has kept up with CTA has been AQR's fund, which has 3X the expenses, uses stock futures (so higher correlation to the S&P), and has a challenging long-term track record.

Since 2010

Let's compare CTA to DBMF and KMLM, which are far more tax-efficient than mutual funds (AMFAX, PQTAX, and AQMIX).

CTA's correlation to the stock market isn't as negative as DBMF and KMLM, but its volatility-adjusted outperformance (alpha) has been the best at 17% annually since its inception.

The upside capture ratio is the best of its peers at -10%.

And the downside capture ratio is the best.

  • If the S&P falls 10%, CTA goes up 8.1%, DBMF 7%, and KMLM 7.3%.

In other words, CTA's correlation to the S&P during correction bottoms (the only correlation regarding hedging) is historically -0.81, slightly better than MLM's -0.77.

That indicates that CTA's approach might be superior, though we'll have to wait at least 40 years to confirm.

Growth ETFs I'll Be Buying In 2025

ETFs aren't just for hedging purposes; they can also provide strong growth above the 13% to 14% cutoff my portfolio uses.

Don't forget that long-term income growth from a portfolio closely tracks long-term total returns.

Historical Income Growth Per $1,000 Investment Since 1998: The Power Of Decades of Dividend Compounding

Metric 12 Exceptional Long-Term Risk Management Aristocrats S&P 500
Total Income $6,298.57 $1,107.61
Inflation-Adjusted Income $3,230.04 $568.01
Starting Yield 4.09% 1.31%
Yield On Cost 77.3% 9.6%
Inflation-Adjusted Yield On Cost 39.63% 4.94%
Income Growth 12.4% 8.5%
Total Return 13.0% 7.0%

(Source: Portfolio Visualizer Premium)

That's because, with annual rebalancing and dividend reinvestment, even non-dividend-paying growth stocks can help boost income growth and thus maximize long-term income.

My "Fantastic Five" are the five Ultra Sleep Well at Night blue chips I have carefully selected due to their low correlation to each other and robust long-term income growth. According to FactSet, the total returns currently have 19% CAGR long-term consensus total returns.

  • My Top 5 Blue-Chip Bargains For October Yield 4.3% With 19% Income Growth Potential
  • 19% CAGR is 5.5% above the Nasdaq's 40-year historical return.
  • Almost 2X the S&P's historical return.

With the S&P now up to a 13% long-term total return consensus, the bar for ETFs to be worth buying has risen.

But this is where my four favorite stock ETFs come in.

The Four Stock ETFs I Like Best For 2025

  1. VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF (VFLO)
  2. Schwab US Large Cap Growth (SCHG)
  3. Return Stacked US Stocks & Managed Futures (RSST)
  4. Return Stacked US Stocks and Yield Futures (RSSY)

VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF: The King Of Deep Value ETFs

VFLO takes the COWZ strategy of free cash flow yield and adds two improvements.

FCF yield (FCF/EV) is the best valuation metric, with the best average returns and the lowest volatility.

First, it uses forward free cash flow instead of trailing. While the future is always less certain than the past, it's also true that FCF yield is often highest for cyclical companies with temporary and mean reverting fundamentals.

In other words, oil companies can have blowout results (like 2021 and 2022), causing FCF yield to soar. However, crashing fundamentals can lead to significant declines.

  • Forward FCF yield minimizes this risk.

VFLO also eliminated the bottom 33% slowest growing companies (consensus growth rates for sales and cash flow), historically boosting returns by 4% annually.

For context, of the 41 fund managers that beat the S&P over the last 21 (top 1.27%), the best one beat the S&P by 1.8% per year. So, 4% outperformance is better than any fund manager's in the last two decades.

VFLO's strategy backtest shows almost 19% annual returns for the last 33 years, Buffett-like returns for a third of a century, from an ETF strategy that requires zero stock picking on the part of investors.

Historical Returns Since 1991

VFLO's strategy (and COWZ's) outperformed Buffett by about 3% to 4% per year.

  • Buffett beat the S&P by 4% for 33 years.
  • FCF yield beat Buffett by 4% for 33 years.

Berkshire's average 12-month rolling return is an impressive 16%, while VFLO's strategy did almost 19%.

19% is the same long-term median consensus for my Fantastic 5 Ultra SWANs.

In other words, VFLO offers the potential to match the Buffett-like returns I'm shooting for with my best stock ideas but with zero stock picking required.

  • And no single stock fundamental risk of going bankrupt.

VFLO's portfolio trades at 12X 2025's free cash flow and just 8X EV/EBITDA (cash-adjusted earnings).

It's a deep-value ETF that targets moderate to strong growth in its companies.

8X cash-adjusted earnings for companies growing at 12%, for a PEG of 0.66. That's not just growth at a wonderful price or Super GARP.

OK, that sounds wonderful. There's 19% return potential from a strategy that proves that "value stocks" have never stopped working for the last 33 years; you just have to measure value correctly using the best valuation metric.

But how about historical returns? What evidence is there that VFLO can deliver Buffett-like returns?

Historical Returns Since July 2023

VFLO is brand new, but its 16-month returns are very impressive. It delivers 26.5% annualized returns, better than the S&P (driven by the Mag 7), value stocks, and COWZ.

VFLO has modestly lower volatility than the S&P (0.85 beta) and delivered 5% annualized alpha, far better than value stocks and COWZ.

Its volatility-adjusted returns were far superior to its value peers and the S&P.

  • Sortino Ratio: Negative-volatility-adjusted excess returns (vs. risk-free bonds).
  • Treynor ratio: Excess total return per unit of volatility.

Value stocks aren't usually significantly less volatile than the S&P but tend to have smaller upside gains in bull markets.

However, VFLO has historically delivered a value-style downside with a 3% better upside than the S&P (upside capture of 103%).

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Expedia Gains On Uber Takeover Report
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Expedia (EXPE) gains after report Uber (UBER) explored potential takeover. (00:24) Meta Platforms (META) laying off employees at WhatsApp, Instagram. (01:28) Be our guest....for a hefty price: Disney (DIS) offering Lightning Lane Premier Pass. (02:19)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE) is up 7% in premarket action after a report that Uber (NYSE:UBER) considered a possible takeover of the company as it tries to diversify its business.

Uber (UBER) talked to advisers in recent months about a bid for Expedia (EXPE) after an acquisition of the company was broached by a third party.

This information is from a Financial Times report late Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

Uber's (UBER) interest was described as being at a very early stage and no deal may come to fruition, according to the report. No formal approach has been made by the ride-sharing company, and there are no current talks.

The focus of Uber's discussions was the role of Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi, who was Expedia’s CEO from 2005 to 2017 and remains a non-executive director, the FT said. Any approach for Expedia would likely be friendly given the CEO's relationship, and he would need to recuse himself from deal talks.

Uber, Expedia and Expedia Chairman Barry Diller declined to comment to the FT.

Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has initiated layoffs across various divisions, specifically targeting teams within WhatsApp, Instagram, and Reality Labs.

Although the exact number of job cuts has not been disclosed, reports indicate that they are relatively small in scale.

“Today, a few teams at Meta are making changes to ensure resources are aligned with their long-term strategic goals and location strategy,” company spokesperson Dave Arnold told The Verge. “This includes moving some teams to different locations, and moving some employees to different roles. In situations like this when a role is eliminated, we work hard to find other opportunities for impacted employees,” he added.

Notably, these layoffs follow a broader trend of workforce reductions at Meta (META), which has seen ~21,000 jobs cut since late 2022 and are a part of the broader cost-cutting initiatives, with CEO Mark Zuckerberg labeling 2023 as the "year of efficiency."

Meta (META) is set to release its Q3 earning results on Wednesday, October 30, after market close.

If you plan on visiting Disney World or Disneyland anytime soon, there’s a chance you’ll spend even more than you originally planned.

Disney (NYSE:DIS) will now let you enjoy the happiest place on earth without waiting in line and without limits.

The company is launching its “Lightning Lane Premier Pass” at its Florida and California theme parks to a limited number of guests at its deluxe resorts. This is not to be confused with the current “Lightning Lane Pass.”

For an additional fee of $137 to $478 (on top of the cost of admission), visitors can enter Lightning Lane attractions without having to book in advance, as is the case with the current Lightning Lane Multi Pass and Single Pass.

Disney World guests can use the pass to enter each attraction once a day, and must be purchased separately for each individual park. Florida’s Disney World will begin the pilot program of the Lightning Lane Premier Pass on October 30 with limited availability, and it’ll be available at California’s Disneyland on October 23.

The extra fee for the Lightning Lane Premier Pass varies based on the theme park and the time of year with the most expensive price of $329-$449 at Disney’s Magic Kingdom and the least expensive price of $129 to $199 at Animal Kingdom.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

Oklo, NuScale Power hit all-time highs on Amazon's plans to go big on nuclear

BlackBerry shows plan to return to profitability next year at investor day

No more shrinkflation? PepsiCo says it will add more potato chips to some bags

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is up 0.3% at $70/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $67,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.7%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is up 7% following the release of better-than-expected Q3 results.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Initial Jobless Claims. The number of individuals filing for unemployment benefits in the U.S. is expected to decline to 241K from 258K, highest level in 14 months.

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales. September retail sales are expected to rise 0.3% over the prior month after gaining 0.1% in August.

  • 10:00 am Housing Market Index. The Housing Market Index is expected to rise for the second month to 43 after gaining to 41 in September.

data.title: Reiterated Strong Buy: Unit Corporation's Leaner Operations Still Look Bullish
data.description:
Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC) has focused on stabilizing its financials and continues to be centered on delivering reliable dividends for its investors. Naturally, the recent dividend cut wasn’t welcomed, but after analyzing the company, I still believe there’s a viable long-term bull case for UNTC. Management seems to be on track as of Q2 2024 despite fluctuating market conditions, reduced revenues, and lower production volumes. However, this was mostly due to the company's ongoing divestitures since 2022. On top of that, the contract drilling segment saw reduced demand and pricing rates, which compounded its challenges. Nevertheless, I think UNTC now has a leaner operation, and as long as revenues stabilize, its dividends seem much more sustainable. Thus, I feel it’s fair to reiterate my “Strong Buy” on UNTC for investors who understand its inherent turnaround risks.

Still an Income Play: Business Overview

Unit Corporation (UNTC) is an energy company with a diversified Oil and Gas portfolio. UNTC extracts oil and natural gas from its properties, acquires properties already in production, and provides contract drilling services to other firms in the sector. UNTC employs specialized rigs and crews to manage its services onshore. Since my February 2023 article, UNTC has remained focused on being an income investment. Even though unfortunately, it had to cut its dividend by half, and its revenues continued declining due to lower production levels.

More recently, UNTC’s oil and natural gas subsidiary reported an increase in average oil prices. Management noted that they saw a 38% rise in Q2 to $79.23 per barrel compared to $57.34 per barrel in the same period in 2023. However, these increments were not enough to compensate for its production volume decrease. You see, oil production dropped by a whopping 49%, and natural gas production fell by 43% YoY in Q2 2024.

Nevertheless, I think it’s worth highlighting that UNTC’s declining production volumes are mostly due to its strategic asset divestitures. These divestitures have been ongoing since 2022, and in 2023, the company shed even more non-core assets. Those core assets were valued at approximately $107 million. UNTC then used those proceeds to bolster its cash reserves and double down on its strategy of focusing on core investments and reducing liabilities. Another positive from this downsizing was that plugging and abandonment costs associated with decommissioning old wells also decreased.

Properties and Financials: Sustainable Dividend Strategy

Furthermore, UNTC’s contract drilling subsidiary reduced its contract pricing and had a lower demand for these services. Despite maintaining the number of available rigs to 14, the average use rate decreased from 15.6 rigs in Q2 2023 to 11.9 rigs in use in Q2 2024. This reduction could be due to competition or external market conditions. Nevertheless, the key factor that affected UNTC’s revenues was the average rig price rates, which declined by 3%, from $31,764 to $30,786.

Currently, UNTC’s main operating area is the Permian Basin in Texas, one of the US’s most oil-productive regions. The company also operates in Oklahoma, including the Anadarko Basin, where its oil and natural gas wells are located. Additionally, UNTC’s Louisiana infrastructure exposes it to another rich oil and gas region. New Mexico is also part of the Permian Basin. Overall, I think UNTC’s remaining assets are located in areas with high production potential that align with the company’s strategy to concentrate on core, abundant regions.

Simultaneously, UNTC decided to keep its quarterly dividend payments at $1.25 per share in Q2 2024. This is key because most of its investor base is undoubtedly looking for a consistent dividend regardless of fluctuating income. The company paid $12.9 million in dividends this quarter, funded by available cash. Moreover, on September 16, the company announced that the quarterly cash dividend for Q3 2024 would also be $1.25 per share.

So, this dividend seems to be the new normal payout going forward. But more importantly, it does corroborate that management remains committed to consistently returning value to its shareholders through regular cash dividends. Additionally, I believe the company seems financially stable and debt-free, so the recent pullback might be a good opportunity for long-term investors.

Relatively Cheap: Valuation Analysis

You see, from a valuation perspective, UNTC now trades at a $318.3 million market cap, making it essentially a microcap now. Its balance sheet holds $80.8 million in cash and equivalents against no long-term debt. However, the company does have about $55.6 million of regular liabilities and a book value of $257.1 million as of Q2 2024. This also indicates a P/B of 1.2. For comparison, its sector’s median P/B is 1.4, so UNTC seems slightly undervalued.

Additionally, over the trailing twelve-month [TTM] period, I estimate that UNTC has generated a positive cash flow of $79.3 million by adding its CFOs and CAPEX. This calculation excludes the $61.2 million it received by selling its PPE over that period, so it’s actually a relatively conservative assessment. Still, I do think it’s worth mentioning that back in Q1 2023, UNTC had accumulated depreciation worth 35.7% of its gross PPE. However, as of Q2 2024, it’s now 45.6% of gross PPE, which suggests that UNTC is investing less in maintenance CAPEX, which might have acted as a cash flow tailwind. Alternatively, it’s also possible that UNTC has sold its less depreciated assets.

Furthermore, UNTC generated $12.1 million in operating income in Q2 2024, which annualized implies a $48.4 million run rate. This would price UNTC today at 6.6 times its operating income, which does seem relatively undervalued. Moreover, I think its high dividend yield seems safe for the foreseeable future as long as revenues stabilize. Therefore, I still lean bullish on UNTC, rating it a “Strong Buy” for long-term income investors who are willing to accept UNTC’s inherent turnaround risks.

Investment Caveats: Risk Analysis

However, the main drawback with UNTC is that its revenues are declining significantly. Since I last covered it back in February 2023, its quarterly revenues have decreased from $93.9 million in Q1 2023 to just $56.8 million in Q2 2024. This is a notable 39.7% decrease, which could very well persist going forward. Moreover, UNTC also cut its quarterly dividend from what used to be $2.50 per share to just $1.25 per share today. Yet, since its stock price is currently around $32.50 today, that’s still a respectable 15.4% annualized dividend yield. The only issue is that this compelling dividend yield holds some risks as long as UNTC’s revenues continue deteriorating.

Furthermore, in their Q1 2024 investor presentation, management mentioned that they were also reducing G&A expenses to reflect their smaller footprint. Thus, for all intents and purposes, UNTC is now adjusting to a much more downsized reality. The only question remains whether or not they’ll continue downsizing if revenues trend even lower or if they’ll stabilize and set a new potential base for future growth around these levels.

Yet, last time I covered UNTC, their SG&A expenses reached $5.1 million in Q1 2023, but as of Q2 2024, they’re now slightly higher at $5.6 million. It’s not a huge increase, but it also raises some questions about their actual downsizing of expenses. Likewise, their gross margins have deteriorated from 53.1% in Q1 2023 to 37.4% in Q2 2024. The same applies to EBIT margins, which declined from 58.0% to 21.3% over the same period.

So, I’m skeptical about management actually reducing expenses to reflect their new, downsized reality. Naturally, it could be explained away by arguing that revenues have declined much faster than expected, outpacing UNTC’s cost-cutting efforts. Thus, as long as revenues stabilize, their cost-cutting strategy will start to reflect improved margins. But this remains speculative until we see that reflected in their actual financials.

Fortunately, the main positive I found about their latest Q2 2024 figures was that UNTC’s latest quarterly cash flow was roughly $18.0 million by adding its CFOs and CAPEX, excluding what it received by selling $3.2 million worth of PPE. Also, it spent $1.1 million on stock repurchases and $12.9 million on paying dividends. Thus, in total, it needs roughly $14.0 million per quarter to sustain this level of stock repurchases and dividend payments. Since it’s already generating $18.0 million in positive cash flow, I think UNTC’s current 15.4% dividend yield seems sustainable. However, this is still contingent on revenues stabilizing.

Still a “Strong Buy”: Conclusion

Overall, UNTC has indeed faced headwinds since I last covered it last year. The main issue is its rapidly declining revenues, which can be explained to some extent due to its downsizing. However, going forward, the main variable to monitor is whether or not revenues stabilize and margins start improving. Fortunately, I think that its current cash flow run rate does suggest its dividend is sustainable at this point. Moreover, the dividend itself somewhat de-risks UNTC as well. So, in total, I think it’s still fair to lean bullish on UNTC, especially now that it’s a more focused operation with an already positive cash flow. Hence, I reiterate my “Strong Buy” rating for income investors who understand its embedded turnaround risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Schrödinger's Path Forward: AI, Cash Burn, And Clinical Uncertainty
data.description:
My last analysis on Schrödinger (NASDAQ:SDGR) was in January 2023. Given its strong revenue growth and promising AI-powered drug discovery platform, my recommendation was to "hold." However, I was concerned about the stock's high valuation and the uncertainty surrounding clinical trial success and profitability. Schrödinger's stock has fallen 64% since then, while the S&P 500 has returned 26%. So it seems like a good time to revisit my analysis to see if Schrödinger's poor stock performance is the result of fundamental changes or simply random chance.

Earlier this year, Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) elected to return the rights of an SOS1 inhibitor back to Schrödinger. The drug had been in IND-enabling stages, but Bristol had gained another SOS1 inhibitor after purchasing Mirati. So the Bristol collaboration, which is focused on a neurology asset, is still ongoing, and Schrödinger is still entitled to receive up to $482 million in development and market milestones (or "biobucks").

In May, Schrödinger's stock struggled after the company released Q1 earnings, which revealed misses on revenue and EPS, with a widening net loss. However, this should not come as a surprise to investors, as Schrödinger's revenues, for now, are entirely dependent on collaborations and clinical trial developments, which are inherently scattered and difficult to predict. Schrödinger's guidance for 2024 remained the same. They projected drug discovery revenue to be between $30 million and $35 million and software revenue growth to be from 6% to 13%.

For Q2, total revenue was $47.3 million, compared to $35.2 million in the second quarter of '23. This was mostly attributed to increases in software revenue, which reached $35.4 million in Q2. The net loss increased to $54 million.

Schrödinger revised its 2024 financial outlook to account for lower gross margins in the software segment. They balanced this by a minor adjustment to their "operating expense growth" forecast from 8-12% to 8-10%.

Schrödinger is also advancing its platform to include toxicity prediction. This, of course, is all aimed at lowering the drug development costs (e.g., avoiding clinical failures due to toxicity).

The company's proprietary pipeline, which includes SGR-1505, is in Phase 1 development. 1505 is a MALT1 (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma translocation protein 1) inhibitor being tested for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. MALT1 is a signaling pathway for cancer cell survival that "drives tumor cell survival, proliferation, and metastasis." 1505 is designed to overcome past safety issues (via higher potency and specificity) of earlier MALT1 inhibitors such as immune suppression, cytokine release syndrome, and hematological toxicity. There are several treatments available for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies (CAR-T cell therapies, bispecific antibodies, BTK inhibitors, and so on), so SGR-1505 will need to deliver very convincing safety and efficacy data to have any chance at market success. Initial clinical data in the Phase 1 dose-escalation study is expected in 1H 2025.

Financial Health

As of June 30, Schrödinger had $108.109 million in cash and cash equivalents. Marketable securities totaled $269.18 million. Total current assets were $449.179 million, while total current liabilities were $99.759 million. This implies a current ratio well over 2, which indicates that Schrödinger can readily cover its short-term obligations. Schrödinger is without significant long-term debts beyond its lease liabilities.

In the first six months of 2024, Schrödinger utilized $92.999 million in operating activities. This implies a monthly cash burn of $15.4 million. If we divide their cash and marketable securities by this figure, it suggests a cash runway of approximately 2 years.

Note: Cash runway and burn estimates are based on historical data and my analysis and should be viewed as estimates only. The term "cash burn" can encompass various factors beyond cash flow, making these estimates somewhat subjective. If companies provide forward-looking estimates in their earnings releases, I will include them to supplement my analysis.

SDGR Stock: Risk/Reward Analysis and Investment Recommendation

The hype surrounding AI-related drug developers like Schrödinger that peaked following NVIDIA (NVDA) investments has waned and the reality of clinical failure has set in. For example, in September, Recursion (RXRX) plunged after disappointing Phase 2 data for its lead asset, REC-994, procured underwhelming data in cerebral cavernous malformation, a rare condition. Although, as I pointed out months before CCM data, 994's disappointing showing was not a surprise. Although AI may have had some role in 994's preclinical development, it was hardly a novel idea, as 994 is a repurposed old compound (tempol). So, I do not believe this was a fair "proof-of-concept" for AI-powered drugs. So far, AI's role in medicine has been to improve success rates in Phase 1 trials, and there is significant value in this. A recent peer-reviewed article described how AI-derived drugs "could have a success rate of 90%-90% above the historical average" because of "algorithmic effects and validated biological targets that reduce the toxicity risk." In this context, Schrödinger's prospects look particularly promising, but it doesn't come without significant operational and financial risks.

Operationally, there are several companies vying for space in AI-enabled drug discovery, even including Google (GOOG). Recursion's setback in Phase 2 demonstrates that clinical development, regulatory approval, and market success are not guaranteed. Schrödinger's own pipeline may experience setbacks of its own. Financially, Schrödinger faces significant net losses as well as high R&D investments required to remain relevant (but possibly insufficient to remain competitive against infinitely larger companies like Google).

In conclusion, SDGR may work as a Quadrant 1 investment in a barbell portfolio, but although Schrödinger's valuation is easier to stomach, there is too much uncertainty to merit a buy rating (maintain "hold").

data.title: Canada Goose: Winter Is Coming, And It's Not Going To Be A Good One (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The stock market doesn’t seem to think that we’re going to enter a recession after a parade of bank earnings showed healthy results. However, not all sectors seem to agree: in particular, the consumer space and luxury goods seem to be continuing to suffer from a pullback in consumer budgets. LVMH, one of the world’s largest portfolios of luxury brands, crashed after reporting weak earnings, blaming a slowdown in Chinese consumption.

Against this backdrop, Canada Goose (NYSE:GOOS) looks incredibly risky as well. The maker of thousand-dollar parka jackets is gearing up for its big winter season, but even in the less-important summer months, negative trends have formed. Yet in spite of brewing challenges, Canada Goose stock has zigzagged between gains and losses this year, settling at down single digits YTD. In my view, there’s a lot more downside left to go.

I last wrote a neutral hold opinion on Canada Goose in June, when the stock was still trading at $13 per share. Since then, the company has released very negative Q2 results, while much more recently, read-through from fellow luxury players like LVMH suggest that a bruising quarter is ahead for Canada Goose - which is quite untimely as the company is expecting to generate three-quarters of its revenue in its fiscal second half of the year, or September through March. With this in mind, I’m downgrading Canada Goose to a sell.

There are three main risks that are clouding my outlook on Canada Goose:

  • Macro malaise and a pullback in luxury spending. Despite an S&P 500 near all-time highs suggesting that we’ve averted the worst from a macro perspective, the same may not be true of all sectors, and consumers are still showing signs of trading down to less-expensive goods to preserve their budgets.
  • Severe decline in wholesale sales. Canada Goose itself is noting that its wholesalers are also tightening their budgets. While Canada Goose has been busy building up its store network and its online presence to boost DTC sales, the company relies on wholesalers to put its brand in front of more customers. Ultimately, fewer wholesalers stocking Canada Goose products means much less brand exposure.
  • China weakness. China results were a bright spot for Canada Goose in Q4, but it seems to have been a temporary blip, with China growth decelerating sharply again. And to hope on any fiscal stimulus from Beijing to support Chinese purchases on foreign luxury goods is probably too optimistic.

Steer clear here: in my view, Canada Goose has a lot more left to lose.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Canada Goose’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q1 (June quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Revenue grew only 4% y/y to CAD $88.1 million. We’ll now go through each of the key issues in turn. The first, as shown in the chart below, is the massive decline in wholesale orders. Wholesale revenue tanked -41% y/y (or -42% y/y on a constant currency basis) to just $16.0 million.

Now, a note here. The summer months are not hugely critical for Canada Goose, as the majority of its revenue still sits in winter jackets, despite its efforts to diversify its product lines into other categories like accessories and footwear. The bright side of the coin here may be that retailers and resellers may not want to stretch their working capital to order Canada Goose jackets as early as the June quarter, if they won’t display them until later in the winter.

However, the weaker sales that have now been ongoing for several quarters suggests that at least some portion of Canada Goose’s B2B buyer universe may be permanently destocking their wares - ultimately the smart choice if budget-conscious consumers aren’t buying its parkas anyway. Recall that Canada Goose’s outlook for FY25, which it affirmed in Q1, calls for wholesale revenue to decline -20% y/y for the full year.

The company notes that it aims to “improve the quality” of the partners that stock Canada Goose products, which may be a euphemism to make Canada Goose appear stronger when some partners are exiting of their own accord. Per CFO Neil Bowden’s remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

This decline reflected our planned lower order book, as we tightened supply to wholesale partners in a soft business environment and the continued optimization of wholesale relationships as we elevate the quality of our partners in this sales channel.

Overall, we're confident in delivery of our order book to wholesale partners for the full year and continue to expect wholesale revenue to decrease 20% year-over-year on a full year basis. We delivered some incremental product in Q1, albeit small, above and beyond the planned order book with select partners.”

Alongside weak channel results, we also note that China revenue slowed considerably. Growth in Q1 in the region was only 11% on a constant currency basis, as shown in the chart below: which compares very poorly against 32% constant currency growth in the preceding Q4. China is already a meaningful (one-quarter) chunk of Canada Goose’s sales, and its fastest growing region by far: so the slowdown here hurts tremendously.

Lastly, to add insult to injury: gross margins in the first quarter declined 540 bps y/y to 59.7%. Make no mistake: a ~60% margin is still quite meaningful for a consumer products company. We note as well that the company cited 330bps of this reduction was self-inflicted, due to supply chain diversification efforts from a recently acquired new factory in Europe, which is producing goods at a higher cost.

However, we note here that the much higher mix of DTC, full-price sales (as opposed to wholesale trade orders at a discounted rate) should be a big benefit to margins, but that impact is being buried. As a result, adjusted EBIT losses widened to CAD -$96 million.

Q2 preview

Canada Goose is expected to report fiscal Q2 (September quarter) results sometime in early November. Now, Q1 was not a great catalyst for the stock, as the aforementioned China slowdown and failure to raise the full-year FY25 outlook didn't do much to appease investor concerns. In my view, Q2 may add further concerns to the mix.

Key items to watch will be deceleration in China, as well as a potential reversion of the U.S. to a revenue decline (versus slight growth in Q1). It's not just Chinese consumers that are pulling back on demand, but amid a potentially more budget-conscious holiday shopping season, many U.S. retailers may be pulling back their order books even further. It's important to note that Canada Goose is expecting a ~20% wholesale decline this year in its outlook: but if Q2 doesn't improve substantially beyond the -42% constant-currency decline that it reported in Q1, investors will be scratching their heads on how Canada Goose will meet its forecast. Again, given the negative read-through from LVMH, it's more likely than not that Canada Goose's Q2 earnings print will be a downside driver for the stock.

Key takeaways

In my view, the clouds are continuing to darken for Canada Goose. In particular, I think the company is already lagging behind several key metrics it set out in its outlook:

The recently affirmed outlook calls for a -20% decline in wholesale revenue (versus a -40% decline in Q1), similar gross margins to FY24 (versus a five point decline in Q1) and 100bps of Adjusted EBIT expansion in FY24 (versus a two point decline in Q1). The bright side of this coin, of course, is to write off Q1 results since they’re the product of a summer quarter that doesn’t contribute much to Canada Goose’s full year results. But in my view, these trends, plus the more recent negative read-through from other luxury industry peers, points to a bleak year ahead for this company.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Rates Spark: Markets Positioned For Back-To-Back ECB Cuts
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker and Benjamin Schroeder

ECB cut most likely, but sharp market positioning prevents the front end going lower

We think the ECB will cut by 25bp, and so do markets, but holding the deposit rate at 3.5% is still possible, in our view. Not cutting would be a big surprise to rates given the implied probability of swaps is north of 90% and has been there for two weeks. After this meeting, markets price in consecutive cuts of 25bp for the next four to come, and thus, a disappointment now would challenge the entire trajectory going forward.

Also, if the ECB cuts as expected, we see a considerable chance that market expectations won’t be met in terms of communication. The ECB will likely want to keep flexibility about a December cut and will push back against the idea that this is the start of consecutive cuts, as is currently priced in. On the other hand, ECB President Christine Lagarde may place more emphasis on mounting growth concerns, which could be caught as a dovish tone by markets. Having said that, we deem it unlikely markets will move to pricing in a probability for 50bp cuts for the time being, and thus, the front-end of the curve should be supported from below.

A pushback against current pricing (or even a decision to hold rates) could see some flattening of the EUR swap curve. Not cutting would risk a broader risk-off move, as worries about a policy error would rise considerably. In this case, the terminal policy rate of the ECB could actually fall back below the neutral range of 2-2.25% as recession risks get priced in more. With little scope for the front end to go lower, we go into the meeting with a bias towards a flatter curve.

Thursday’s events and market view

A cut from the ECB is seen as a done deal, not just looking at market pricing. All 37 economists surveyed by Bloomberg see a cut. If delivered, the cut itself could be a non-event for markets, and the focus will mainly fall on communication with no new ECB forecasts to be presented this time.

US data should become more influential again. For retail sales, the strong auto sales should provide a good baseline, and our economists look for 0.4% MoM growth given decent consumer credit card borrowing numbers. Industrial production holds some downside after weak survey data. Markets tend to be more sensitive to the jobless claims, which nudged higher last week. Hurricanes and knock-on effects of strikes have led to some volatility, but this time a stable level is anticipated.

Primary markets will see Spain in the market reopening 7Y to 24Y bonds for up to €5.5bn. France will also be in the market selling shorter-dated bonds out to the 5y maturity and linkers, up to €14.5bn in total.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
